lead: When a business moves, the desks and chairs are the least of your worries. It is the computers that must come back to life, the records that must stay confidential and complete, and the furniture that has to arrive without a scratch that really decide whether the move feels smooth or fraught. Our crews have relocated countless Suffolk businesses, and below we share what we have learned about moving the three things that matter most: your IT, your files and your furniture.

sections.h: Why IT, Files and Furniture Need Different Plans
sections.body:
It is tempting to treat an office move as one big lift, but the three core elements behave very differently and each needs its own approach. IT is fragile, time-critical and the lifeblood of the business. Files are physically simple but carry real responsibility around confidentiality and order. Furniture is bulky and heavy but, handled well, the most forgiving of the three. Lump them together and something always suffers.

In our experience, the moves that go wrong do so because everything was packed in the same rush, with the server boxed alongside the kitchen mugs and confidential files thrown loose into open crates. The fix is simple: plan each element separately, sequence them sensibly, and give the trickiest, the IT, the most attention and the earliest start.

Thinking this way also helps you decide what to keep. A move is the ideal moment to retire ageing computers, shred files you are no longer required to hold, and let go of furniture that has seen better days. Less to move means a faster, cheaper relocation and a cleaner start, so a little honest sorting before you pack pays off handsomely.

sections.h: Moving IT Without Losing a Day
sections.body:
Technology is the part of the move that can genuinely cost you trading days, so it deserves the earliest and most careful planning. Start by talking to your IT support and providers as soon as the new address is fixed. New broadband or leased lines often take weeks to install, so order them early and aim to have the connection live and tested before staff arrive rather than waiting hopefully on the day.

Before anything is unplugged, back everything up. A full, verified back-up means that even in the worst case your data is safe, and it lets everyone work with confidence. Photograph the back of machines and racks before disconnecting cables, so reconnection at the new site is a matter of matching the picture rather than guesswork. Label every cable, port and device clearly by destination.

Computers, screens and especially servers need careful handling and proper padding in transit; these are not items to be stacked loose in a van. Our crews treat them as the delicate, valuable equipment they are. Plan the order of reconnection too, bringing core systems up first, and have your IT team on site at the new premises to test everything before the working day begins. Done this way, the technology need cost you barely a pause.

sections.h: Handling Business Files and Records Securely
sections.body:
Paper records and physical files look like the easy part of a move, but they carry a quiet responsibility that catches firms out. Many businesses hold documents containing personal or commercial information, and that data must stay confidential and secure throughout the move. Loose files in open crates, left unattended in a hallway, are exactly the kind of slip that causes a problem nobody wants.

Pack files into sturdy, sealed crates rather than open boxes, keep related records together, and label each crate clearly so you can find things quickly at the other end without rifling through everything. For sensitive material, keep a simple inventory of what has been packed and where, so nothing can go astray unnoticed. The aim is that every record is accounted for from the moment it is boxed to the moment it is shelved again.

A move is also the right time to deal with records you no longer need to keep. Anything past its required retention period can be securely destroyed rather than carried to the new office, which lightens the load and reduces what you are responsible for. Whatever you do keep, transport it in a planned, supervised way, and confirm on arrival that every crate is present before you sign the move off.

sections.h: Protecting Furniture and Equipment in Transit
sections.body:
Office furniture is the heavy, bulky end of a business move, and while it is more forgiving than IT or files, it still rewards proper handling. Desks, cabinets and conference tables are easily scuffed, glass tops are easily cracked, and a filing cabinet moved with its drawers full is both dangerous and likely to fail. The trick is preparation: empty, dismantle and protect before anything is carried.

Empty desks and cabinets completely so they are lighter and safer to lift, and so nothing rattles loose inside. Dismantle large items such as desks and modular workstations where it makes them easier to move, keeping the fittings bagged and labelled with the piece they belong to so reassembly is quick. Our crews wrap and pad furniture properly for the journey, but knowing in advance what comes apart helps the day run faster.

Take particular care with the awkward and the valuable: glass-topped tables, tall storage units, anything with sharp corners or a delicate finish. Tell us about these in advance so they travel with the right protection. At the new premises, having a clear floor plan means furniture goes straight to its final position and is reassembled in place, rather than being shuffled around the office while staff try to work.

sections.h: Sequencing the Move for Minimal Disruption
sections.body:
How you order the move matters as much as how you pack it. The goal is to have the business functioning again as quickly as possible, which means thinking carefully about what arrives and is set up first. Generally, IT and the key workstations should be among the first items unloaded and reconnected, so essential staff can be operational while the rest of the office takes shape around them.

Plan the load with the unload in mind. The things you need first should come off the lorry first, so it pays to load them last. Files can usually follow once the core systems are up, and bulkier furniture can be positioned around the working areas as it arrives. A move sequenced this way feels purposeful rather than chaotic, and gets people back to work in the right order.

Keep someone in charge at each end, checking items against an inventory and directing the crew to the right rooms. In our experience, a short, well-planned spell of disruption is far better than a long, muddled one, and the businesses that sequence their move thoughtfully are usually the ones trading again the soonest, with the fewest things gone missing along the way.

sections.h: Settling In and Checking Everything Works
sections.body:
Once the lorry has gone, the final task is to confirm that the three things you cared about most have all made the journey intact. Bring your IT systems fully online and test them properly: network, email, phones, shared drives and any specialist software your business relies on. Catching a problem in the first hour, with your IT team still on site, is infinitely easier than discovering it mid-morning with customers waiting.

Check your files next. Confirm that every crate has arrived, that sensitive records are accounted for, and that everything is shelved back in good order rather than left stacked in a corner to deal with later. The sooner records are properly filed at the new premises, the sooner normal work resumes and the lower the risk of anything being mislaid.

Finally, walk the furniture. Make sure desks and cabinets are reassembled, positioned to the plan, and undamaged, and address any snags promptly while the move is fresh. Get IT, files and furniture all settled in these first days and your business move is genuinely complete, with your people working comfortably and confidently in their new Suffolk home from the very start.

faqs: How do I move office IT without losing data?

Take a full, verified back-up before anything is unplugged, photograph the back of machines so cables reconnect correctly, and label every device by destination. Order new broadband early so it is live before staff arrive, and have your IT team test everything on arrival.
How should confidential business files be moved?

Pack them into sturdy, sealed crates rather than open boxes, keep related records together, and label each crate clearly. Maintain a simple inventory of sensitive material so nothing goes astray, and never leave files unattended in a hallway during the move.
Do I need to empty filing cabinets and desks before moving?

Yes. Empty them completely so they are lighter and safer to lift and nothing rattles loose. A filing cabinet moved with full drawers is both dangerous and likely to fail. Bag and label any fittings from dismantled furniture so reassembly is quick at the other end.
What should be set up first at the new office?

IT and the key workstations, so essential staff can work while the rest of the office is arranged around them. Load those items last so they come off the lorry first, then bring files and bulkier furniture into place once core systems are running.
